content:
# Genesis Chapter 46 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 27r–27v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_046_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_046_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), 4th century CE — LXX | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0050 (page 46), https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0051 (page 47)

---

## The Last Night Vision

**1** Israel set out with everything he had. He came to Beersheba, and there he offered sacrifices to the God of his father Isaac. **2** God spoke to Israel in visions of the night and said, "Jacob! Jacob!" And he said, "Here I am." **3** He said, "I am God — the God of your father. Do not be afraid to go down to Egypt, for I will make you into a great nation there. **4** I myself will go down with you to Egypt, and I myself will surely bring you back up again. And Joseph — his own hands will close your eyes."

---

## The Departure

**5** Then Jacob set out from Beersheba, and the sons of Israel carried their father Jacob, their little children, and their wives in the wagons Pharaoh had sent to carry him. **6** They took their livestock and their possessions, which they had accumulated in the land of Canaan, and they came to Egypt — Jacob and all his offspring with him: **7** his sons and his sons' sons with him, his daughters and his sons' daughters — all his offspring he brought with him to Egypt.

---

## The Seventy: Those Who Came to Egypt

**8** These are the names of the sons of Israel who came to Egypt — Jacob and his sons:

Reuben, Jacob's firstborn.

**9** The sons of Reuben: Hanoch, Pallu, Hezron, and Carmi.

**10** The sons of Simeon: Jemuel, Jamin, Ohad, Jachin, Zohar, and Shaul — the son of a Canaanite woman.

**11** The sons of Levi: Gershon, Kohath, and Merari.

**12** The sons of Judah: Er, Onan, Shelah, Perez, and Zerah — though Er and Onan had died in the land of Canaan. And the sons of Perez were Hezron and Hamul.

**13** The sons of Issachar: Tola, Puvah, Jashub, and Shimron.

**14** The sons of Zebulun: Sered, Elon, and Jahleel.

**15** These are the sons of Leah, whom she bore to Jacob in Paddan-aram, together with his daughter Dinah. All his sons and daughters numbered thirty-three persons.

**16** The sons of Gad: Ziphion, Haggi, Shuni, Ezbon, Eri, Arodi, and Areli.

**17** The sons of Asher: Imnah, Ishvah, Ishvi, and Beriah, and their sister Serah. The sons of Beriah: Heber and Malchiel.

**18** These are the sons of Zilpah, whom Laban gave to his daughter Leah. These she bore to Jacob — sixteen persons.

**19** The sons of Rachel, Jacob's wife: Joseph and Benjamin.

**20** To Joseph in the land of Egypt were born Manasseh and Ephraim, whom Asenath daughter of Potiphera, priest of On, bore to him.

**21** The sons of Benjamin: Bela, Becher, Ashbel, Gera, Naaman, Ehi, Rosh, Muppim, Huppim, and Ard.

**22** These are the sons of Rachel who were born to Jacob — fourteen persons in all.

**23** The son of Dan: Hushim.

**24** The sons of Naphtali: Jahzeel, Guni, Jezer, and Shillem.

**25** These are the sons of Bilhah, whom Laban gave to his daughter Rachel. These she bore to Jacob — seven persons in all.

**26** All those who went with Jacob to Egypt — those who were his own flesh and blood, not counting Jacob's sons' wives — were sixty-six persons in all. **27** And the sons of Joseph who were born to him in Egypt were two. All the persons of the house of Jacob who came to Egypt were seventy.

---

## Judah Goes Ahead

**28** Jacob sent Judah ahead of him to Joseph, to prepare the way before him to Goshen. And they came to the land of Goshen. **29** Joseph harnessed his chariot and went up to meet his father Israel in Goshen. He presented himself to him and fell on his neck and wept on his neck — a long time.

**30** Israel said to Joseph, "Now I can die — since I have seen your face and know that you are still alive."

---

## The Strategy for Goshen

**31** Joseph said to his brothers and to his father's household, "I will go up and tell Pharaoh, and I will say to him, 'My brothers and my father's household, who were in the land of Canaan, have come to me. **32** The men are shepherds — they are keepers of livestock — and they have brought their flocks and their herds and everything they have.' **33** So when Pharaoh calls you and asks, 'What is your occupation?' **34** you are to say, 'Your servants have been keepers of livestock from our youth until now — both we and our fathers.' That way you will be able to settle in the land of Goshen, because every shepherd is an abomination to the Egyptians."

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### "I am God, the God of your father" — the Last Divine Speech to a Patriarch

**אֲנִי הָאֵל אֱלֹהֵי אָבִיךָ** — "I am God, the God of your father."

This is the last time God speaks directly to any of the great Genesis patriarchs. After this night vision at Beersheba, the curtain falls on direct divine speech to Abraham's line until Moses at the burning bush in Exodus 3. The weight of that silence deserves attention.

God's self-identification here is careful and layered. He calls himself **הָאֵל** (*ha-El*, "the God," using the singular *El* with the definite article) and then adds **אֱלֹהֵי אָבִיךָ** (*Elohei avikha*, "the God of your father") — the relational anchor. This same construction appeared when God spoke to Jacob at Bethel in Genesis 28:13, when Jacob fled from Esau: "I am the LORD, the God of your father Abraham and the God of Isaac." The formula reaches back through the generations, anchoring the present moment in the covenant chain. Now, as Jacob prepares to leave the land of the promise entirely — to go down to Egypt, the direction of slavery, not ascent — God shows up at the border with this formula: *the God of your father.*

The command is **אַל-תִּירָא** (*al-tira*, "do not be afraid") — the same two words spoken to Hagar in the wilderness (Genesis 21:17), to Abraham before the covenant ceremony (Genesis 15:1), to Isaac at Beersheba in a parallel theophany (Genesis 26:24), and to Jacob himself at multiple crisis points. The words are not a platitude; they are the signature of divine presence at moments of maximum danger. The danger here is real: to leave Canaan is to leave everything the covenant has been oriented toward. God's "do not be afraid" acknowledges the legitimate weight of what Jacob is doing.

And then the closing promise — **יוֹסֵף יָשִׁית יָדוֹ עַל-עֵינֶיךָ** (*Yosef yashit yado al-einekha*, "Joseph will place his hand upon your eyes") — is perhaps the most tender thing God says to any patriarch. The closing of the eyes was the son's task in ancient Israelite death customs: the act of the most beloved who stays until the end. God is not merely promising survival; God is promising that Jacob will die attended by the son he mourned for twenty years. The reunion will be long enough to matter. Jacob will not simply glimpse Joseph and then die in shock — he will live long enough in Egypt to have Joseph there at the last moment, hand on his eyes, closing them gently. This is what "I will bring you back up" means: not just the eventual return of Israel's bones (Genesis 50:25), but the present assurance that the departure into Egypt is not abandonment.

---

### Yarad / Alah — the Geometry of Egypt

The chapter is structured around two verbs: **יָרַד** (*yarad*, to go down, to descend) and **עָלָה** (*alah*, to go up, to ascend). Egypt is consistently "down" in the Genesis geography — a descent from the highland land of promise. Canaan is "up." This vertical metaphor carries enormous theological freight.

God says in verse 4: **אָנֹכִי אֵרֵד עִמְּךָ מִצְרַיְמָה** (*anokhi ered imkha Mitsraymah*, "I myself will go down with you to Egypt") — using the emphatic personal pronoun **אָנֹכִי** (*anokhi*, "I, I myself") for stress. God does not merely say *I will go down*; the *anokhi* insists on God's personal, first-person accompaniment. This is not a delegation or a promise of general providential care — it is God personally going down to Egypt with Jacob.

Then immediately: **וְאָנֹכִי אַעַלְךָ גַם-עָלֹה** (*ve'anokhi a'alkha gam-aloh*, "and I myself will surely bring you up again"). The **גַם-עָלֹה** (*gam-aloh*) is an infinitive absolute construction used for emphasis, the Hebrew equivalent of italics: "I will *absolutely surely certainly bring you up*." The descent to Egypt is not the end of the story. The direction of the narrative is temporarily downward but ultimately upward.

This vertical geography — down to Egypt, up to Canaan — will organize the entire book of Exodus. The Exodus is not simply a political liberation; it is an *ascent*, a coming back up from the descent that Genesis 46 initiates. Jacob's going-down is what makes Moses's going-up meaningful.

---

### Beersheba — the Sacred Border

Jacob does not rush to Egypt. He stops at Beersheba first to sacrifice. Beersheba (Hebrew: **בְּאֵר שָׁבַע**, *Be'er Sheva*, "the well of seven" or "the well of oath") is the southernmost extent of the promised land — the boundary marker of Israel's territory, hence the idiom "from Dan to Beersheba" for the full extent of the land. Before Jacob crosses the border into Egypt, he stops at the threshold and offers sacrifices.

The sacrifices are offered **לֵאלֹהֵי אָבִיו יִצְחָק** (*le-Elohei aviv Yitschak*, "to the God of his father Isaac") — a specific, personal invocation. Isaac too had been at Beersheba; God had appeared to Isaac there (Genesis 26:23-25), and Isaac had built an altar and called on God's name there. Jacob is not just stopping at any spot — he is stopping at his father's altar-place, his father's sacred boundary. He is placing himself in the line of faithful departure and return. This is not abandonment of the covenant; it is an act of covenant faithfulness before crossing into the unknown.

The divine response comes at night: **בְּמַרְאֹת הַלָּיְלָה** (*bemaroth halaylah*, "in visions of the night"). The plural *maroth* (visions) suggests a sustained visionary experience, not a single flash. The night-vision genre in Genesis always occurs at moments of maximum vulnerability and transition: Jacob's ladder at Bethel (Genesis 28), his wrestling at the Jabbok (Genesis 32), Joseph's dreams (Genesis 37, 40, 41). God speaks into the dark, into the uncertain night before the crossing.

---

### "Joseph will close your eyes" — the Promise of a Good Death

**יוֹסֵף יָשִׁית יָדוֹ עַל-עֵינֶיךָ** — "Joseph — his hand will he place upon your eyes."

The word order in the Hebrew is emphatic: *Yosef* comes first, the name fronted for stress. "Joseph — his hand will he place upon your eyes." Not some Egyptian attendant. Not a servant. Joseph.

The gesture of closing the eyes of the dead was a final act of intimacy and honoring performed by the nearest loved one. In Egyptian practice, there were elaborate rituals of preparation of the body, but the closing of the eyes at the moment of death was the most immediate act — done before the body growers cold, while the dying person might still sense the presence of the one performing it. For Jacob, who has mourned Joseph as dead for over two decades, who has lived in the grief of a father who outlasted what he believed to be his son, this promise is almost unbearably specific. God is not saying *Joseph will still be alive when you die.* God is saying *Joseph will be there at the bedside. Joseph's hand will be the last thing you feel.*

The fulfillment of this promise is recorded in Genesis 50:1: *And Joseph fell upon his father's face and wept upon him and kissed him.* The text does not specify the hand upon the eyes — but the presence, the weeping, the kiss — these are the substance of what God promised at Beersheba.

---

### "Now let me die" — Fulfilled Longing, Not Despair

**אָמוּתָה הַפָּעַם אַחֲרֵי רְאוֹתִי אֶת-פָּנֶיךָ** — "Let me die now, this once, since I have seen your face."

Jacob's words at the reunion (v. 30) are not a death wish in any pathological sense. The cohortative **אָמוּתָה** (*amutah*, "let me die, I would die, now I may die") expresses not desire for death but the satisfying of the ultimate longing. The logic is: *I have seen you; I can die.* Everything I have been holding on for — the reason I refused to let go even in grief — has now arrived. I can release my grip.

The phrase **הַפַּעַם** (*hapa'am*, "this time, now, this once") is an intensifier that marks the moment as definitive. Not "I could die eventually" but "right now, at this moment, if death came, it would be enough." This is the speech of completion, of satiety after long hunger.

Compare Jacob's other great declarations: in Genesis 32:26, wrestling with the angel, he says **לֹא אֲשַׁלֵּחֲךָ כִּי אִם-בֵּרַכְתָּנִי** (*lo ashallechakha ki im berachtani*, "I will not let you go unless you bless me") — the fierce grip of a man who refuses release until he has received what he needs. Here, the grip loosens. He has received. He can let go. The father who would not be comforted (Genesis 37:35) — **וַיְמָאֵן לְהִתְנַחֵם** (*vayema'en l'hitnahem*, "he refused to be comforted") — is now comforted beyond anything he imagined. The comfort did not come in the form he had expected. It came through Egypt, through famine, through Judah's speech, through wagons sent from a foreign court. But it came.

The reunion weeping in verse 29 — **וַיֵּבְךְּ עַל-צַוָּארָיו עוֹד** (*vayyevk al-tsavvarav od*, "he wept on his neck still/for a long time") — uses the adverb **עוֹד** (*od*, "still, yet, again, more, a long time") in a striking way. Joseph fell on his father's neck and wept and *kept weeping*, wept and did not stop. The verb is a narrative imperfect describing extended, continuing action. This is not a brief embrace. Jacob and Joseph hold each other at the neck and weep — and the weeping goes on. The text refuses to rush it. Twenty-two years of separation does not resolve in a brief sob.

---

### Toevat Mitsrayim — the Abomination of Egypt

**כִּי-תוֹעֲבַת מִצְרַיִם כָּל-רֹעֵה צֹאן** — "because every shepherd is an abomination to the Egyptians."

The word **תּוֹעֵבָה** (*toevah*) is one of the strongest terms of revulsion in the Hebrew vocabulary. It denotes not merely dislike or distaste but ritual, moral, or social defilement — the thing that causes revulsion on an almost visceral level. The term is used in Leviticus and Deuteronomy for prohibited sexual practices, for idolatry, for the consumption of unclean animals, for dishonest commercial practices — always at the strongest end of the prohibitive register. Here it is used for Egyptian attitudes toward shepherds.

This Egyptian revulsion toward herders has several historical explanations that may underlie the text. Egyptian records express consistent disdain for Semitic pastoralists entering Egypt from the east — the *Shasu* and *Aamu* peoples who came in through the Sinai with their flocks were regarded as alien, unclean, and threatening. Egyptian wall paintings and inscriptions from the Middle Bronze Age consistently depict Canaanite herdsmen as ethnically and socially inferior. Some scholars connect this passage with the Hyksos period, when Semitic rulers controlled Lower Egypt — the word "Hyksos" itself deriving from the Egyptian *heka khasut* ("rulers of foreign lands") — and Egyptian memory of foreign pastoral domination created a cultural revulsion that became encoded as *toevah*.

Joseph's advice is sociologically shrewd: by identifying as shepherds — which is what they are, and which is a *toevah* to Egyptians — the family of Israel will be assigned to Goshen, the eastern delta region, away from the Egyptian heartland, in a kind of voluntary segregation that will allow them to maintain their identity and their way of life without assimilation into Egyptian culture. The very characteristic that makes them abominable to Egyptians will be the instrument of their preservation as a distinct people. The *toevah* becomes a protective barrier.

---

### The Genealogy: Seventy into Egypt, Seventy-Five in the LXX

**כָּל-הַנֶּפֶשׁ הַבָּאָה לְיַעֲקֹב מִצְרַיְמָה יֹצְאֵי יְרֵכוֹ... שִׁבְעִים** — "all the persons who came with Jacob to Egypt, those who came from his loins... seventy."

The number **שִׁבְעִים** (*shiv'im*, seventy) in verse 27 is the Masoretic Text's explicit count. The genealogy in verses 8–27 is carefully structured into four household groups with subtotals:

- **Leah's sons** (vv. 8–15): 33 persons
- **Zilpah's sons** (vv. 16–18): 16 persons
- **Rachel's sons** (vv. 19–22): 14 persons
- **Bilhah's sons** (vv. 23–25): 7 persons

Total of descendants: 33 + 16 + 14 + 7 = 70. Verse 26 specifies 66 persons of Jacob's own blood (excluding Joseph, Manasseh, and Ephraim, who were already in Egypt, and excluding the wives of Jacob's sons). Verse 27 then adds Joseph and his two sons (already in Egypt) to arrive at 70 total.

The number **seventy** carries symbolic weight throughout the biblical literature. Seventy elders of Israel (Exodus 24:1), seventy nations in the Genesis 10 table of nations, seventy years of exile in Babylon (Jeremiah 29:10) — the number functions as a number of fullness, completion, and totality. "Seventy souls" going down to Egypt is a complete household, a whole nation in seed form.

The Septuagint, however, counts **seventy-five**. The LXX adds five additional names to the genealogy — specifically, it expands Joseph's Egyptian family by adding three sons of Manasseh and two sons of Ephraim (unnamed in the MT), citing them as born to Joseph in Egypt, drawn from Numbers 26 and 1 Chronicles. This LXX count of 75 is specifically cited in the New Testament: in Acts 7:14, Stephen's speech before the Sanhedrin says, "Joseph sent and invited his father Jacob to come, along with all his relatives, seventy-five persons." Stephen is reading from the LXX. The MT/LXX divergence here is thus a significant textual crux that runs from Genesis 46 directly into the New Testament. Neither number is an "error" — they represent two distinct textual traditions, each internally consistent, each counting by different rules of inclusion.

---

## Manuscript Observations and LXX Variants

**Vaticanus page structure:** The Vaticanus page pa_0046 (GEN_046_vaticanus_page_001.jpg) carries the bulk of Genesis 46 in its characteristic three-column uncial layout. The dense genealogical section (vv. 8–27) is clearly visible occupying the majority of the page, with names rendered in continuous uncial script without word breaks. The chapter marker visible at the upper left — **46** — confirms page placement. Significantly, page pa_0047 (GEN_046_vaticanus_page_002.jpg) opens with the illuminated decorated initial **T** (in red and gold ornamental uncial) and the word **ΕΞΟΔΟC** (*Exodos*, Exodus) clearly visible at the top — the *Exodus* book-heading — confirming that Genesis 46 concludes on or at the very top of pa_0047, and the remainder of that page is occupied by Exodus 1. The transition from Genesis to Exodus occurs on a single manuscript page in Vaticanus, without a full page break — the two books separated only by the book-title heading.

**LXX expanded genealogy (vv. 20, 27):** In verse 20, the Vaticanus text significantly expands the MT's brief notice about Manasseh and Ephraim. The LXX adds that Manasseh fathered Machir (father of Gilead) and that Ephraim fathered Shuthelah (whose son was Tahath), and inserts additional sons of Ephraim, arriving at five additional persons for the Egyptian-born branch of Joseph's family. This expansion accounts for the LXX total of 75 vs. the MT's 70. The MT's verse 20 reads simply: "To Joseph in the land of Egypt were born Manasseh and Ephraim, whom Asenath daughter of Potiphera, priest of On, bore to him." The LXX's version is several lines longer, drawing genealogical information from Numbers and Chronicles and inserting it here.

**The divine speech (vv. 2-4):** For **אֲנִי הָאֵל אֱלֹהֵי אָבִיךָ** (*ani ha-El Elohei avikha*, "I am God, the God of your father"), the LXX reads **ἐγώ εἰμι ὁ θεὸς τῶν πατέρων σου** — "I am the God of your fathers" (plural: *pateron*, fathers). Where the MT specifies only Isaac (*avikha*, "your father," singular — specifically Isaac, last mentioned in the chain), the LXX generalizes to "the God of your fathers" (plural), encompassing the entire patriarchal chain. This is a theologically minor but textually interesting difference: the MT is more precise (this is Isaac's God — the specific link in the immediate chain), while the LXX is more inclusive (this is the God of all your forefathers).

**"I will go down with you" (v. 4):** For **אָנֹכִי אֵרֵד עִמְּךָ** (*anokhi ered imkha*, "I myself will go down with you"), the LXX reads **ἐγὼ καταβήσομαι μετά σου εἰς Αἴγυπτον** — "I will go down with you into Egypt." The Greek preserves the first-person emphasis but does not capture the emphatic force of the Hebrew *anokhi* as well as the Hebrew allows. The MT uses *anokhi* (the long-form emphatic "I") where the shorter *ani* would have been grammatically sufficient — the choice of the emphatic form is deliberate and theologically loaded. The LXX renders it with *ego* (which can be emphatic but is the ordinary Greek first-person pronoun), slightly softening the personal force of the Hebrew.

**"Joseph will close your eyes" (v. 4):** The LXX renders **יוֹסֵף יָשִׁית יָדוֹ עַל-עֵינֶיךָ** as **Ιωσηφ δὲ ἐπιβαλεῖ τὰς χεῖρας αὐτοῦ ἐπὶ τοὺς ὀφθαλμούς σου** — "And Joseph will place his hands upon your eyes." The LXX uses the plural *cheiras* (hands), where the MT has the singular *yado* (his hand). This is a minor variant — perhaps reflecting a slightly different gesture, or simply the LXX translator generalizing — but the MT's singular "his hand" is more intimate: not "the hands" performing a ritual act, but one hand, placed tenderly.

**"Every shepherd is an abomination" (v. 34):** For **כִּי-תוֹעֲבַת מִצְרַיִם כָּל-רֹעֵה צֹאן** (*ki-toevat Mitsrayim kol-roeh tson*, "because every shepherd is an abomination to the Egyptians"), the LXX reads **βδέλυγμα γάρ ἐστιν Αἰγυπτίοις πᾶς ποιμήν προβάτων** — "for every shepherd of sheep is a *bdelygma* to Egyptians." The Greek word **βδέλυγμα** (*bdelygma*, abomination, detestable thing, something causing disgust) is the Septuagint's standard rendering of **תּוֹעֵבָה** (*toevah*) throughout the Hebrew Bible. It is the same word used in LXX for the "abominations" of Leviticus, the "detestable things" of Deuteronomy, and — in the New Testament — for the "abomination of desolation" (*bdelygma eremoseos*) in Matthew 24:15 and Daniel 9:27. The LXX here preserves the full strength of the Hebrew: no softening, no gentler term. Shepherd = *bdelygma* = abomination.

---

## Structural Note

Genesis 46 has a distinctive three-panel structure that moves from intimate to bureaucratic, with the emotional climax at its center:

**Panel 1 — The Last Theophany (vv. 1-7):** Jacob at the border, the night vision, God's speech, the departure. This panel is intimate, vertical (God to human), and future-oriented. It answers the question no one has spoken: *Is this leaving of the land right?* God's answer is yes — and I will go with you and bring you back.

**Panel 2 — The Genealogy (vv. 8-27):** The enumeration of seventy persons. This panel is horizontal, collective, and past-oriented — accounting for every soul who came. In the midst of a narrative of reunion and journey, the genealogy pauses time and takes inventory. Its effect is to establish that what is going down to Egypt is not just one man's family but a whole people in seed form. These seventy names are the book of Exodus waiting to happen.

**Panel 3 — The Reunion and Strategy (vv. 28-34):** Judah as forerunner, the meeting at Goshen, Joseph weeping on Jacob's neck, Jacob's words "now let me die," and Joseph's strategic briefing. This panel is again intimate — the reunion that has been the goal of chapters 37–46 — and then turns practical: how will this family survive in Egypt? The answer involves their *toevah* status as shepherds. What marks them as outsiders will mark them as a people.

The name-alternation Jacob/Israel throughout the chapter is precise: the patriarch is "Jacob" when he acts in the natural, familial register (setting out, sacrificing, meeting Joseph at Goshen) and "Israel" when he acts in the covenantal, national register (God addresses him as Jacob then Israel in the theophany, verse 2; Joseph goes to meet "Israel in Goshen," verse 29; "Israel said to Joseph," verse 30). The interplay is intentional: this is a man who carries two names because he is two things simultaneously — a grieving father named Jacob and the founding patriarch of a nation named Israel. Both are going down to Egypt. Both will come back up.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 27r–27v, West Semitic Research Project (Bruce E. Zuckerman). Folio 27r (GEN_046_page_001.jpg) carries Genesis 46:1 through the middle of the genealogy (approximately vv. 1–21). Folio 27v (GEN_046_page_002.jpg) carries the remainder of the genealogy and the closing narrative (approximately vv. 22–34). Secondary reference: Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana, pages 46–47 (pa_0046–pa_0047); accessible at https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0050 and https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0051. Note: Vaticanus pa_0047 begins the book of Exodus immediately following the conclusion of Genesis 46, with the illuminated book-title heading ΕΞΟΔΟC visible at the top of the page.*

---

*— PNEUMA*
